summary: I was born in Katase, Izu, which is located in Shizuoka Prefecture, Japan. Growing up there, I witnessed the effects of the Russo-Japanese War through my relatives who fought. Katase was part of my early life before I left Japan as a teenager in 1909 to join my father in America.

I was born in Shizuoka Prefecture, specifically in Katase, Izu. It is a region in Japan where my family lived before I moved to the United States. My early memories of Japan, including the Russo-Japanese War, are tied to this area.

I remember the Russo-Japanese War well from my childhood in Japan. My relative went to fight in that war, and I recall Japan's victory with pride. The peace treaty was signed in Newport, Rhode Island, and President Roosevelt was involved in the negotiations.

The Treaty of Portsmouth was the peace agreement that ended the Russo-Japanese War. I remember it was signed in Newport, Rhode Island, and that President Theodore Roosevelt played a key role. This event was significant in my early memories of Japan's history.

Theodore Roosevelt was the U.S. president during the time the Treaty of Portsmouth was signed to end the Russo-Japanese War. I remember hearing about his involvement in negotiating peace between Japan and Russia. His role was notable in the history I learned growing up in Japan.

During World War I, I volunteered for the United States Army even though I was not a citizen. I was studying electrical engineering at Cal-Tech when the institution was turned over to the Army. As an engineer, I learned how to build bridges and participated in drills while continuing my studies.

I attended the California Institute of Technology, or Cal-Tech, majoring in electrical engineering. It was unusual for a Japanese person to be in college at that time, and I was the only one. While studying there during World War I, the school was turned over to the U.S. Army, and I volunteered to serve while continuing my education.

I graduated from Pasadena High School in 1915 after moving to Pasadena in 1911. Before that, I briefly attended Royal High School in San Francisco. At Pasadena High, I was active in school activities and made many friends despite some early challenges with discrimination.

I was born and raised in Japan, specifically in Katase, Izu, Shizuoka Prefecture. I left Japan as a teenager in 1909 to join my father who was already in America. My early life in Japan included memories of the Russo-Japanese War, schooling, and exposure to both Buddhist and Christian influences.

Shimoda was near where I attended high school in Japan, and it was significant because it was the first port opened to Americans. This early contact meant we received Christian teachings earlier than most people in Japan. I occasionally attended church there and heard sermons from a Japanese minister.

My father studied at Keio University, which was known as Keio Gijuku then. However, he left school when my grandfather lost money in the shipping business and came to America to make money. Keio University was part of my family's educational background in Japan.

Before coming to America, my brother and I spent time at the Yokohama pier talking to foreigners, which helped me practice English. This experience made it easier for me to adjust when I arrived in the U.S. I also recall that the immigration law was already in effect when I left Japan from Yokohama.

I traveled to America aboard the Shunyo Maru, which was the best steamship at that time. I had an introduction to the captain through a relative, and I was treated very well during the voyage. The ship stopped in Hawaii, but I did not get off because I had someone waiting for me in America.

During my trip to America on the Shunyo Maru, we stopped in Hawaii where several passengers disembarked. I noticed people swimming even in winter and divers retrieving coins we threw from the ship. However, I did not get off in Hawaii since I had family waiting for me in the United States.

I landed in San Francisco when I arrived in America in 1909. The city was still recovering from the earthquake, with the Fairmont Hotel being one of the few big buildings standing. I lived with a friend of my father and started school and work there before moving to Pasadena.

When I arrived in San Francisco in 1909, the Fairmont Hotel was the only big building that had been reconstructed after the earthquake. It stood out in a city that was still largely torn down. This image stayed with me as part of my first impressions of America.

While attending school in Pasadena, I regularly went to a Presbyterian Church located on Madison and Colorado streets. The church was an important part of my social and religious life, and I often attended with the families I worked for. Unfortunately, the church building has since been torn down.

I attended Los Angeles Junior College for a while and played tennis on the team. The girls from Pasadena High School supported me enthusiastically. During this time, I also worked for a city attorney's family, balancing school, sports, and work.

Before graduating from Cal-Tech, I sought work through the Los Angeles Examiner newspaper, which was helping veterans find jobs. Unfortunately, they told me they could not find a white collar job for me due to prejudice. This experience showed me the challenges I faced despite my education.

I moved to Pasadena in August 1911 to live with my uncle after my father returned to Japan. I attended Pasadena High School and later Cal-Tech there. Pasadena was where I built many friendships, attended church, and started my adult life in America.

Seattle was not directly mentioned in my story, so I do not have personal experiences or memories related to that city. Most of my life in America was centered around San Francisco, Pasadena, and Los Angeles.
